name: Management Interface Exposure and Enumeration
description: An operator or adversary discovers and profiles exposed management interfaces used to administer infrastructure, facility, telecoms, OT, or security platforms. Enumeration often includes login portals, API endpoints, version banners, default paths, and weak authentication controls that can turn a planning surface into an initial-access surface.
detection_notes: Look for unusual authentication failures, scanning against admin paths, and management-origin traffic from unexpected sources.

name: Shared Operator Credential Reuse
description: Attackers exploit shared, default, or weakly rotated operator accounts that span multiple systems or sites. Once a reused admin credential is obtained, the same trust path can often unlock routers, controllers, facility systems, or orchestration tooling across otherwise separate categories.
detection_notes: Flag logins where the same account suddenly touches multiple device families or disparate environments in a short window.

name: Provisioning, Policy, or Template Tampering
description: An attacker alters approved templates, neighbour lists, schedules, rule sets, or provisioning records so that future changes carry malicious state by default. This is effective because the poisoned template is later pushed by trusted operators or automation rather than by obviously hostile actions.
detection_notes: Watch for template changes outside maintenance windows and drift between approved baselines and deployed state.

name: Remote Support Tunnel Hijack
description: Attackers hijack or impersonate trusted support paths used by vendors, field engineers, or managed-service providers. Because these channels are often pre-approved and poorly inspected, they can provide quiet access into telecoms sites, OT environments, data-centre facilities, or physical security systems.
detection_notes: Correlate vendor sessions against change tickets, expected source ranges, and maintenance authorisations.

name: Jump Host or Bastion Pivot
description: A compromise of a bastion or jump host turns a control point into a pivot hub across many hardware and software types. The attacker can inherit operator reach into routers, hypervisors, BMS servers, engineering workstations, timing appliances, or subscriber systems without exposing themselves at each downstream asset.
detection_notes: Monitor for unusual downstream fan-out from bastions and session origin mismatches between user context and target type.

name: Third-Party Maintenance Workflow Impersonation
description: Rather than directly exploit a device, the attacker abuses the business process that grants trusted maintenance access. Fake work orders, social-engineered approvals, or modified dispatch notes can unlock physical entry, vendor remote access, or temporary admin rights across multiple technology categories.
detection_notes: Validate maintenance requests against asset ownership, approved vendors, and known contact paths rather than ticket text alone.

name: Service Account or API Token Replay
description: Stolen machine credentials or API tokens are replayed against trusted control paths that span hardware and software. This commonly affects orchestration layers, telemetry collectors, DCIM, OSS, provisioning systems, and any environment where background services are allowed broad non-interactive access.
detection_notes: Track token use by expected source, scope, and time pattern; service identities should behave far more consistently than humans.

name: Secrets Vault or PAM Abuse
description: Once a secrets broker is abused, attackers can harvest the privileged credentials and certificates that glue together different infrastructure layers. That can collapse the separation between IT, OT, facility, and telecom control planes because the broker itself already understands the full trust map.
detection_notes: Alert on bulk secret access, unusual vault search behavior, and check-outs that do not align with planned work.

name: Certificate or Trust Anchor Substitution
description: Attackers replace or add certificates, roots, or device trust anchors so that malicious systems are accepted as legitimate peers. This can enable MITM positions, rogue network functions, false management endpoints, or malicious timing sources depending on the environment.
detection_notes: Track unauthorized CA changes, unexpected certificate chains, and sudden trust of previously unknown peers.

name: CI/CD Pipeline Poisoning
description: An adversary compromises the path that builds, signs, packages, or publishes updates to operational systems. The poisoned release then lands through normal maintenance workflows and can affect routers, controllers, VMS appliances, hypervisors, telecom functions, or OT software alike.
detection_notes: Correlate pipeline changes, signing events, and release content against approved source commits and maintainer identities.

name: GitOps or Configuration Drift Injection
description: Instead of logging into devices directly, the attacker changes the source of truth so automation deploys malicious or destabilizing state on their behalf. This is effective wherever configuration-as-code governs cloud platforms, BMS schedules, network policy, telecom CNFs, or privileged operational settings.
detection_notes: Diff deployed state against approved branches and alert when automation pushes large multi-system changes without corresponding change records.

name: Malicious Firmware, Image, or Package Update
description: The attacker pushes a modified firmware image or software package through a trusted update channel or maintenance action. The target may continue to function normally while gaining backdoor logic, weakened validation, or a latent kill switch that can be exercised later.
detection_notes: Verify package provenance, monitor unexpected downgrade or rollback events, and hash-check staged update bundles.

name: Segmentation or ACL Manipulation
description: An attacker alters the boundaries that are supposed to separate management, production, safety, subscriber, or facility networks. This does not always cause immediate outage, but it quietly creates future paths for reconnaissance, pivoting, or direct control traffic where none should exist.
detection_notes: Alert on policy changes that broaden source scope, management reachability, or east-west access between previously isolated zones.

name: Routing or Path Steering Manipulation
description: By changing routing state, path preference, or failover behavior, attackers can divert sensitive traffic through controllable points, create asymmetric outage conditions, or selectively isolate monitoring and control systems. The same idea applies across enterprise, telecom, OT, and facility transport layers.
detection_notes: Compare route changes and failover events against expected maintenance windows and passive path baselines.

name: Out-of-Band Console or Serial Access Abuse
description: Attackers target supposedly isolated recovery paths that are often less monitored than the primary management plane. OOB or serial access can bypass network controls and provide direct administrative reach into routers, servers, cameras, facility controllers, and industrial endpoints.
detection_notes: Monitor OOB use as a high-signal event and correlate it with declared incidents or hands-on maintenance activity.

name: Alarm Flooding and Operator Desensitization
description: Rather than disable detection outright, the attacker drowns operators in noisy but plausible events so that the truly important signal is missed or delayed. This is effective in any environment where humans must triage alarms under time pressure and where suppressing the alert volume would itself be suspicious.
detection_notes: Look for sudden correlation breakdown, bursty repeated alerts from many assets, and operators forced into broad alarm suppressions.

name: Log, Historian, or Evidence Suppression
description: The attacker deletes, overwrites, blocks, or diverts the evidence trail needed to reconstruct what happened. Different environments implement this differently, but the objective is consistent: destroy ordered records so defenders cannot distinguish fault, misuse, and intentional manipulation.
detection_notes: Alert on silent collectors, abnormal drops in ingest volume, missing recorder retention, and unexplained gaps in event sequences.

name: KPI, Sensor, or Telemetry Falsification
description: Instead of attacking the control path directly, the adversary manipulates the observed state so operators believe conditions are normal, degraded for the wrong reason, or improving when they are not. False visibility can protect a longer sabotage sequence against both humans and automated remediation.
detection_notes: Cross-check telemetry against independent sources and flag impossible or inconsistent state combinations.

name: GNSS Jamming and Timing Denial
description: An attacker degrades or denies satellite timing so dependent systems fall back to holdover or lose synchronisation entirely. This impacts any environment where lawful timing, event ordering, radio synchronisation, or protection coordination rely on an external time source.
detection_notes: Monitor GNSS signal quality, holdover transitions, antenna faults, and correlated timing alarms across dependent systems.

name: GNSS Spoofing or False Time Injection
description: Rather than deny timing outright, the attacker feeds a believable but false time source so dependent platforms accept bad time as good time. This can quietly corrupt record integrity, lawful audit trails, synchronisation, or multi-system correlation without causing immediate obvious failure.
detection_notes: Use multi-source time validation and alert on drift, clock-class changes, or impossible offsets between peer systems.

name: PTP Grandmaster or Boundary Clock Manipulation
description: The attacker tampers with the devices that distribute trusted time inside the network, allowing incorrect time to propagate through otherwise healthy systems. This is especially dangerous where many downstream devices assume the timing fabric is authoritative and do not validate it independently.
detection_notes: Track clock source changes, profile changes, and abrupt offset propagation from a small number of timing distribution nodes.

name: Badge Cloning and Access Workflow Abuse
description: An attacker clones, borrows, or fraudulently obtains access credentials to reach equipment or control rooms that are assumed to be physically trustworthy. Once inside, they can target almost any technology stack regardless of whether the logical control plane is well segmented.
detection_notes: Correlate badge use against expected person-location patterns, escort requirements, and simultaneous-use anomalies.

name: CCTV Blindness and Recorder Tampering
description: The adversary disables, mis-aims, saturates, or desynchronizes the surveillance system that should verify movement and incident reconstruction. That creates the freedom to perform physical tampering, asset swaps, or unauthorised maintenance while reducing confidence in later investigations.
typical_effects: Delayed perimeter or room intrusion detection
detection_notes: Alert on camera tamper events, sudden loss of streams, recorder retention changes, and time skew across camera fleets.

name: BMS or HVAC Setpoint Manipulation
description: Attackers change environmental control logic, schedules, or setpoints so the facility behaves incorrectly while operators initially interpret the problem as mechanical drift or sensor failure. This can impact compute environments, telecom shelters, industrial spaces, and security rooms that depend on stable environmental conditions.
detection_notes: Monitor for unexpected setpoint changes, mode flips, and command sequences that do not align with ambient conditions or approved schedules.

name: UPS, PDU, or Power Controller State Manipulation
description: The attacker manipulates the digital control layer of the power chain rather than physically cutting power. By changing thresholds, transfer logic, outlet state, runtime assumptions, or alarm handling, they can trigger disruptive electrical behavior while keeping the issue rooted in a trusted control system.
detection_notes: Correlate controller commands with electrical telemetry and investigate unplanned transfer or outlet-state changes immediately.

name: PLC, Controller, or Logic Parameter Change
description: A trusted engineering or configuration path is used to change parameters or executable logic inside controllers. The specific hardware varies widely, but the underlying attack type is consistent: modify the logic that governs physical or supervisory behavior while keeping the device online.
detection_notes: Baseline controller projects and alert on unauthorized uploads, downloads, or parameter deltas.

name: Engineering Workstation Tool Abuse
description: The attacker abuses the legitimate software that operators and engineers use to program or commission the environment. These tools often bypass normal production guardrails because they are designed specifically to make deep changes to devices, controllers, radios, and supervisory platforms.
detection_notes: Treat engineering-tool launches and project transfers as high-signal events, especially outside maintenance windows.

name: Fieldbus or Legacy Protocol Impersonation
description: Attackers inject or impersonate protocol traffic in environments where authentication is weak, optional, or absent. Even when the exact device type differs, the same attack logic applies: speak a trusted control or telemetry protocol well enough to issue commands or falsify state.
detection_notes: Use protocol-aware monitoring to identify new talkers, unexpected function codes, and command sequences from non-standard hosts.

name: Backup, Failover, or Restoration Sabotage
description: The adversary prepares the target for a worse future incident by degrading the mechanisms that are meant to restore service. That may mean corrupting backups, breaking failover preconditions, invalidating spare configurations, or modifying recovery documentation so the outage lasts longer and defenders make worse choices.
detection_notes: Audit backup integrity, failover readiness, and recovery configuration drift as continuously as primary production controls.

name: Safety Interlock or Protection Bypass
description: The attacker alters the conditions that should halt unsafe behavior or reveal abnormal operation, either by suppressing a trip path or by abusing inhibit and maintenance modes. The exact equipment differs between environments, but the cross-domain pattern is the same: remove the last line of defense before other manipulation occurs.
detection_notes: Track every bypass, inhibit, and protection-setting change as an emergency-level event with explicit approval and timed revalidation.
